info.title: getting started
info.text: In this demo we'll use SketchRNN an RNN (Recurrent Neural Network) created by Google Magenta trained on millions of Quick Draw doodles, to generate drawings stroke by stroke in the browser. Each time you click the button, the model samples a new, unique doodle from what it learned.

info.title: libraries
info.text: We import the netnet standard library nn, and @magenta/sketch, which exposes the ms global.

info.title: global vars
info.text: We create a few global variables, model holds the SketchRNN instance, button is the draw button (we keep a reference so we can update its label and disable it while drawing), and canvas is where the strokes are rendered.

info.title: setup
info.text: The setup function runs on page load. It points the model at a pre-trained checkpoint of model weights for cat doodles hosted on Google Cloud Storage. The model will get downloaded to our browser and run locally the first time we call drawCat

info.title: canvas styles
info.text: We create a button wired to drawCat and a 500×500 canvas. We configure the canvas drawing style: black strokes with a lineWidth of 3, and rounded lineCap and lineJoin so the stroke ends and corners look natural, like a pen or pencil rather than sharp geometric line segments (which is how canvas draws lines by default)

info.title: guard and lazy init
info.text: The drawCat function first checks whether it's already running: if the button already says '...drawing...' we return early to prevent stacking multiple generations. Otherwise we update the button and call model.initialize() if the model hasn't been loaded yet, waiting for it before continuing.

info.title: starting state
info.text: We call model.setPixelFactor(2), after clearing the canvas, to scale the coordinates up so the drawing isn't tiny. The starting pen position is set to the center of the canvas. model.zeroState() returns the initial hidden state of the model, an empty "memory" the model will build on as it generates each stroke. prevPenDown starts at 1 (pen down) so the first stroke is drawn.

info.title: the generation loop
info.text: We loop up to 120 times, generating one stroke per iteration. model.getPDF(state, 0.2) computes a probability distribution over possible next strokes given the current state. The 0.2 is the temperature: lower values make the model more conservative (closer to the most likely strokes), higher values add more randomness and variety. model.sample(pdf) then picks a stroke from the distribution list of possible next strokes.

info.title: update state
info.text: We pass the sampled stroke back to model.update(stroke, state), which advances the model's hidden state so the next iteration knows what happened before. The stroke is then destructured into its components: dx and dy are the relative movement from the current position, penDown tells us whether the pointer is touching the canvas, and penEnd signals the model wants to stop.

info.title: drawing the stroke
info.text: We compute the next position by adding the deltas to the current position. If prevPenDown was 1 (pointer was down on the previous step), we draw a line from the old position to the new one. Then we update the current position and save the current penDown state for the next iteration.

info.title: end condition and sleep
info.text: If penEnd === 1 the model is signaling that the drawing is complete and we break out of the loop. Otherwise we await nn.sleep(20) (a 20 millisecond pause) before the next stroke. This slows down the rendering just enough to give the drawing a satisfying animated quality rather than snapping in all at once.

info.title: lots more
info.text: The Quick Draw dataset contains over 100 subjects, cats are just one of them. Try changing the URL to point at a different model (e.g. dog.gen.json, bicycle.gen.json). You can also experiment with the temperature value: try 0.6 or 0.9 to see how randomness changes the character of the drawings.
